quickInsight: God called Moses — a guy who stuttered and felt completely unqualified — to do one of the biggest jobs in history, which means He can absolutely use you exactly as you are right now.
keyThemes: God knows you by name and has a specific mission for you
Feeling unqualified doesn't mean you are — God fills in the gaps
Standing up for what's right even when it's risky or unpopular
Your identity matters more than your circumstances
God is patient with our doubts and questions
discussionQuestions: Moses gave God like five excuses for why he couldn't do what God asked (Exodus 3–4). What excuses do we make when we feel prompted to do something hard — talk to the new kid, bear our testimony, stand up for someone?

When God said 'I AM THAT I AM' (Exodus 3:14), He was basically saying His identity doesn't depend on anyone else's opinion. How would it change the way you handle peer pressure if you really knew who you were and didn't need anyone else to validate it?

Moses saw an Israelite being mistreated and stepped in (Exodus 2:11-12), but he handled it the wrong way. When you see something wrong — bullying, someone being left out, cheating — what does it look like to stand up the RIGHT way?

The Israelites had been slaves so long they almost forgot they were meant for something greater (Exodus 6:9). Are there things in your life — habits, friend groups, screen time — that might be keeping you from seeing what you're actually capable of?

God spent 40 years preparing Moses in the wilderness before the burning bush moment. How do you think the boring, everyday stuff you're doing right now might be preparing you for something you can't see yet?
teachingIdeas: OBJECT LESSON — The unqualified tool: Bring a beat-up, old tool (a dented wrench, a cracked phone, etc.) and ask if they'd pick it to do an important job. Then point out that God picked a stuttering shepherd hiding in the desert to free an entire nation. God doesn't call the qualified — He qualifies the called. Ask: what's your 'stutter' that you think disqualifies you?

ACTIVITY — The excuse wall: Give everyone a sticky note. Have them write one excuse they've used to avoid doing something hard (talking to someone, trying out, bearing testimony). Stick them on the wall. Read Moses' five excuses from Exodus 3–4 and compare. Then rip them down together — God's answer to every excuse was basically 'I'll be with you.'

REAL TALK — Identity under pressure: Read Exodus 3:14 ('I AM THAT I AM'). Talk about how God's identity doesn't shift based on who's watching. Then ask: are you the same person at school, online, at church, and at home? Or does your 'identity' change depending on who's around? Use a sports analogy — the best players don't change their game based on the crowd; they play their game every time.

STORY CONNECTION: Share a quick story about someone (athlete, missionary, everyday person) who felt completely unprepared but stepped up anyway. Connect it back to Moses at the burning bush — the moment God says 'I'm sending you' and Moses says 'Who, me?' That's the same feeling every missionary gets, every new quorum president gets, every guy who decides to be different from the crowd.
activities: COURAGE CHALLENGE: Give each young man a simple but specific challenge for the week — sit with someone at lunch who's alone, compliment someone they normally wouldn't talk to, or put their phone down for one full evening and be present with their family. The next week, share what happened. Connect it to Moses: small acts of courage prepare you for bigger ones.

BURNING BUSH HIKE (mutual activity): Go on a night hike or walk and stop at a meaningful spot. Read Exodus 3:1-6 about Moses seeing the burning bush. Discuss: God spoke to Moses in an unexpected, quiet place — not in a crowd. Ask them where they've felt the Spirit strongest. Challenge them to find their own 'burning bush' spot this week — a place they can go to think and listen.

IDENTITY SHIELDS: Have each young man create a simple shield (paper, cardboard) divided into four sections: something they're good at, something they believe, someone who counts on them, and something God might be preparing them for. Based on Exodus 3-4 — Moses didn't see his own potential, but God did. Display them in the quorum room as a reminder.
